perturbation.original_text: The parties shall be entitled to exchange Proprietary Information under the <PAGE> terms of this Agreement for a period not to exceed two (2) years from the date hereof, unless otherwise extended by mutual written agreement of the parties or incorporated into a separate agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties shall be entitled to exchange Proprietary Information under the <PAGE> terms of this Agreement for a period not to exceed one (1) year from the date hereof, unless otherwise extended by mutual written agreement of the parties or incorporated into a separate agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the exchange period for proprietary information from two years to one year. This contradicts the implied assumptions in the rest of the agreement, especially Section 1, where the limitations of obligations are defined. The two-year period implicitly provides a standard baseline for business development activities before obligations shift.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (i), (ii), (iii) and (v) shall not apply with respect to all obligations after five (5) years following the disclosure thereof or any information that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally known to the public, (i) was in its possession or the possession of an affiliate or consultant or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it, an affiliate or a consultant by a third party without restriction, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party by employees of the Receiving Party who can be demonstrated to have had no access to such information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the initial agreement period for exchanging proprietary information from two years to one year. This directly contradicts the contradicted text which defines obligations based on a five-year period following disclosure. Since the first clause now defines a shorter period for proprietary information exchange, it undermines the five-year period defined in the contradicted text, thus affecting the limitations of obligations defined in the contradicted section, and creating a strong inconsistency regarding the applicable timeframe. This fits the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where conflicting deadlines or obligations are defined.

perturbation.original_text: Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (i), (ii), (iii) and (v) shall not apply with respect to all obligations after five (5) years following the disclosure thereof or any information that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally known to the public, (i) was in its possession or the possession of an affiliate or consultant or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it, an affiliate or a consultant by a third party without restriction, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party by employees of the Receiving Party who can be demonstrated to have had no access to such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (i), (ii), (iii) and (v) shall not apply with respect to all obligations after three (3) years following the disclosure thereof or any information that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally known to the public, (i) was in its possession or the possession of an affiliate or consultant or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it, an affiliate or a consultant by a third party without restriction, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party by employees of the Receiving Party who can be demonstrated to have had no access to such information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time after which obligations no longer apply from five years to three years creates an in-text contradiction. The period after which the clause applies does not correlate to the 2 years in the upper section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties shall be entitled to exchange Proprietary Information under the <PAGE> terms of this Agreement for a period not to exceed two (2) years from the date hereof, unless otherwise extended by mutual written agreement of the parties or incorporated into a separate agreement. Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (i), (ii), (iii) and (v) shall not apply with respect to all obligations after five (5) years following the disclosure thereof or any information that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally known to the public, (i) was in its possession or the possession of an affiliate or consultant or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it, an affiliate or a consultant by a third party without restriction, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party by employees of the Receiving Party who can be demonstrated to have had no access to such information.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a five-year period after which certain obligations no longer apply. The changed text reduces this period to three years. The contradicted text states the parties shall be entitled to exchange Proprietary Information for a period not to exceed two (2) years. Therefore, the reduction from five to three years creates a clear inconsistency: the agreement states the parties will exchange info for 2 years, while the obligation to protect the info extends for 5 years originally, now only 3 years. This creates a direct conflict within the contract, making enforcement unclear.

perturbation.original_text: Immediately upon (i) the decision by either ------------------- party not to enter into a relationship as a result of the exchange of information hereunder, or (ii) a request by the Disclosing Party at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts or derivatives thereof to the extent it is requested by either party in writing, except that a single copy may be retained for legal archival purposes, subject to protection and non-disclosure in accordance with the term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Immediately upon (i) the decision by either ------------------- party not to enter into a relationship as a result of the exchange of information hereunder, or (ii) a request by the Disclosing Party after 6 months, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts or derivatives thereof to the extent it is requested by either party in writing, except that a single copy may be retained for legal archival purposes, subject to protection and non-disclosure in accordance with the term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the phrase "after 6 months" to the return request clause creates a contradiction. The clause previously stated the Receiving Party had to return proprietary information upon request "at any time." Adding a time constraint on when a return can be requested is contradictory and affects the entire premise of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party understands that nothing herein (i) requires the disclosure of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party, which shall be disclosed if at all solely at the option of the Disclosing Party, or (ii) requires the Disclosing Party to proceed with any proposed transaction or relationship in connection with which Proprietary Information may be disclosed. 4. No Publicity. Except to the extent required by law, neither ------------ party shall disclose the existence or subject matter of the negotiations or business relationship contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Disclosing Party to request proprietary information back 'at any time'. The changed text introduces a 6-month delay on that request. The contradicted text states that the Disclosing Party has the option to disclose Proprietary Information, or proceed with any proposed transaction or relationship, at any time, 'solely at the option of the Disclosing Party'. By adding the 'after 6 months' limitation, the Changed Text is in direct conflict with the Contradicted Text, and limits the Disclosing Party's options.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be held by a court or other tribunal of competent jurisdiction to be to any extent illegal, invalid or unenforceable, such provisions shall be limited or eliminated to the minimum extent necessary so that this Agreement shall otherwise remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be held by a court or other tribunal of competent jurisdiction to be to any extent illegal, invalid or unenforceable, such provisions shall be considered null and void and removed in their entirety from this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the provision related to unenforceable clauses of the contract, there is an in-text contradiction. This new change conflicts with the earlier part of the same section, which states that provisions should be limited to the 'minimum extent necessary'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that ------------- due to the unique nature of the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any breach of its obligations hereunder, that any such breach may allow the Receiving Party or third parties to unfairly compete with the Disclosing Party resulting in irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the Disclosing Party may seek appropriate equitable relief (without the need to post bond or other security) in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party in writing immediately upon the occurrence of any such unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware. In the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be held by a court or other tribunal of competent jurisdiction to be to any extent illegal, invalid or unenforceable, such provisions shall be limited or eliminated to the minimum extent necessary so that this Agreement shall otherwise remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that if any provision is deemed unenforceable, it should be limited or eliminated to the minimum extent necessary. The changed text states that such provisions should be considered null and void and removed entirely. The contradicted text expects the original clause, which requires the provision to be limited to the minimum extent necessary, to remain valid. The change directly contradicts this requirement because the new provision mandates complete removal, thus leading to a strong in-text contradiction according to the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware creates a direct conflict and increases the chances of disputes arising from this in-text contradiction. Due to the conflicting state laws, it is impossible to determine which state law will be applied to the Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or validity of this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts in and for the County of San Diego, California, and each party hereby agrees to submit to the personal and exclusive jurisdiction and venue of such courts. This Agreement supersedes all prior discussions and writings and constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. This Agreement may not be amended except in an express writing signed by officers of both parties. No waiver or modification of this Agreement will be binding upon either party unless made in writing and signed by a duly authorized representative of such party and no failure or delay in enforcing any right will be deemed a waiver.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from California to Delaware. The contradicted text specifies that disputes will be subject to the jurisdiction of courts in San Diego, California. Since the governing law has been changed to Delaware, there is a direct conflict regarding which state's laws and courts have jurisdiction, creating a strong in-text contradiction. This adheres to the definition of inconsistencies leading to in-text contradictions.
